nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish tributaries, side arms, and forest lakes of the lower Amazon basin; often associated with submerged roots, fallen branches, overhanging riparian vegetation, and leaf litter

decorPreferences: dense planting (fine-leaved plants, Java moss, water sprite)
driftwood and leaf litter to replicate blackwater biotope

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality micro-pellets and flake food as a daily staple. Color and condition improve with regular offerings of live or frozen foods: daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, mosquito larvae, and Moina. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily; avoid overfeeding as this species is prone to obesity in captivity.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetra, ember tetra, neon tetra)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving species that may outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Female specimen photo credited to Kornobis, S., as shown on the FishBase summary page for Megalamphodus bentosi.

summary: A graceful, soft-water tetra from the lower Amazon basin of Brazil, the Ornate Tetra features a pinkish-silver body, boldly white-tipped dorsal and anal fins, and a distinctive dark operculum spot that distinguishes it from the closely related Rosy Tetra (H. rosaceus). Easy to keep and long established in the hobby.
careNotes: The Ornate Tetra is one of the most long-standing and widely kept small tetras in the hobby, commercially bred in large numbers and readily available. It thrives in groups of 8–10 or more; smaller groups suppress natural shoaling behaviour, cause stress, and lead to drab colouration. A tank of at least 75 L with a 90 cm footprint accommodates a proper shoal with room for midwater swimming. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–12) best reflects its native blackwater habitat and brings out full coloration, though commercially bred specimens are more adaptable and tolerate neutral conditions. Avoid hard, alkaline water for long-term keeping. A heavily planted setup with dark substrate, driftwood, and leaf litter is strongly preferred; bright open tanks with light substrate wash out coloration and increase skittishness. Gentle filtration maintaining good oxygenation without strong current is ideal. The species is often confused with the Rosy Tetra (H. rosaceus, fw-fish-219): the key field mark is a dark vertical spot just behind the operculum (gill cover) present in H. bentosi and absent in H. rosaceus. Most fish sold in the trade as “Rosy Tetra” are actually H. bentosi. Males are slimmer with more vivid fin markings; females are deeper-bodied. See also: [rosy-tetra] (H. rosaceus — lacks operculum spot, native to Guyana/Suriname).
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; moderate breeding difficulty. Condition a group with live and frozen foods before selecting the most colourful males and plump females for a separate, dimly lit breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, temperature 26–28°C). Fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) serve as egg substrate. Spawning typically occurs in the morning; parents must be removed immediately to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming after 4–5 days. Feed fry infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, progressing to freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Light must be kept very dim during the first week as fry are photosensitive.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hyphessobrycon bentosi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided type locality (Óbidos, Pará, Brazil), water parameters (20–28°C, pH 5.0–7.5, hardness 18–215 ppm), adult size (40–45 mm SL), tank minimum (90 × 30 cm base), diet (omnivore; dried + live/frozen), temperament (peaceful), group size (8–10+), habitat description, and breeding notes. Also confirmed the operculum-spot distinction from H. rosaceus.

additional.name: FishBase — Megalamphodus bentosi
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (4.3 cm SL), temperature (24–28°C), pH (5.8–7.5), hardness (5–19 dH), taxonomy (Characiformes), native range (Amazon River basin, South America), and minimum tank length (60 cm). Image credit: Kornobis, S.

additional.name: Aquarium Store Depot — Ornate Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade care guide. Confirmed temperature 22–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, gH 1–12, kH 1–8, minimum tank 75 L (20 gal), group size 8–10+, lifespan 3–5 years, breeding egg-hatch 24–36 h, fry free-swimming 3–4 days, and good/avoid tankmate lists.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Bentosi Tetra
additional.notes: UK trade source. Confirmed adult size to 5 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness up to 20 dH, peaceful temperament, group of 6–10+ recommended, and diet (flake, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia). Also noted sexual dimorphism: females larger and fuller-bodied, males brighter.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Hyphessobrycon rosaceus (Rosy Tetra)
additional.notes: Used to verify the distinguishing feature between H. bentosi and H. rosaceus: the dark operculum spot is present in H. bentosi and absent in H. rosaceus. Also confirmed geographic separation (H. rosaceus: Guyana, Suriname; H. bentosi: lower Amazon, Brazil).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer requiring separate soft-acidic breeding tank (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5), fine-leaved plant cover, and dim lighting. Remove parents immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch 24–36 h; fry free-swimming at day 4–5. First food: infusoria, then brine shrimp nauplii.
